feedback.text: Im a long-time gamer and dual citizen of Norway and North America. Ive spent years supporting the games industry. Mainly through steam and other digital platforms in recent years, but also as a kid growing up with consoles like the NES, Mega drive, SNES etc. Ive connected with friends, and supported titles that shaped my life to some extent. ( Deus ex 1 is still my favourite immersive sim. ). But in recent years, Ive seen more and more games I bought disappearnot because I deleted them, but because publishers decided to shut down their servers. When that happens, I lose access to something I paid for. Something I valued. And theres nothing I can do. I dont particularly like that. And it seems i am not alone in this. I support the Stop Killing Games initiative because I believe this is a basic issue of fairness. If we pay for a game, we should be able to play iteven after official support ends. Whether its an online multiplayer title or a single-player game with artificial server requirements, the principle is the same: removing access to legally purchased content is unethical. Im asking the EU to take a clear stand. This Digital Fairness Act is the perfect place to start. Please: Require that games with server dependencies offer an offline mode or option for private servers once official support ends. Prevent companies from using EULAs to override a consumers right to access what they paid for. Recognize games as part of our cultural heritageworthy of the same preservation standards as books, films, and music. We are not asking for free games. We are asking for the right to keep what we bought. Thank you for hearing me and for helping shape a digital future that respects ownership, creativity, and fairness. Med vennlig hilsen! Norwegian gamer
